title: "Big error on startup"
date: 2010-05-19
forum: General Help

### Post by ali999 on 2010-05-19
Hi

I was using lucid lynx just fine when it froze for no apparent reason and I had to restart my computer. On restarting, I now get this error:

```


Mount: mounting /dev/disk/by-uuid/<numch of hexadecimal number> on /root failed: Invalid argument
mount: mounting /dev on /root/dev failed: No such file or directory
mount: mounting /sys on /root/sys failed: No such file or directory
mount: mounting /proc on /root/proc failed: No such file or directory
Target filesystem doesn't have /sbin/init/
No init found. Try passing init= bootarg.

BusyBox v1.13.3 (Ubuntu 1:1.13.3-1ubuntu11) built-in shell (ash)
Enter 'help' for a list of built-in commands

(initramfs) _


```

However the computer is frozen at this point. I cannot type anything and am forced to restart manually only to get the same message again and again. Fortunately I have a dual boot setup and was able to load into windows and post this. 

I am not quite sure what to make of this, and will be quite disappointed if I have reinstall ubuntu just for this. Would greatly appreciate any help on this issue.

---

### Post by ali999 on 2010-05-19
Alright then....since im getting no help here, next question

Does anybody know of a way to load ext4 formatted drives in windows so that I can backup all my stuff and then reinstall linux?

---

### Post by john newbuntu on 2010-05-19
For your original problem, please try and run a filesystem check and see if it fixes the problem.   You need to boot from an Ubuntu liveCD/liveUSB, choose the try ubuntu option.
Open up a terminal (apps->accessories->terminal) and run:
sudo fdisk -l
This will give you your devices.  Identify all your "Linux" devices and run e2fsck on those.  

For example, if /dev/sda5 is a Linux device, run:
sudo e2fsck -C0 -p -f -v /dev/sda5
If this gives any errors, then run:
sudo e2fsck -f -y -v /dev/sda5

---

Just so other people see this I got the same error after the latest update about 2hrs before posting this. I am trying the fix listed above right now and will post again to let people know if it helps.

---

### Post by john newbuntu on 2010-05-20
Good for you that you got it to work.  
Perhaps somebody who knows the kernel/filesystem well can explain each error in detail.  But generally speaking, your filesystem had run into consistency problems resulting in it getting into an unclean state.  Among these errors, you had an inode(file or directory metadata) in a superblock (metadata information about filesystem) that was invalid and several counts that were off.

So essentially, you repaired these problems non-interactively by running a repair tool called "e2fsck".  Now that you are able to login, you might want to make a complete backup of your data (or even entire partitions) and check the health of your hard disk drive.  Also make sure that you power off gracefully.  It could be that the freeze that you experienced caused all these problems.  But it is hard to tell without knowing the entire picture.

---

Im not an expert but clearly there are a lot of errors that keep appearing on the filesystem for no apparent reason. I am certain its not the disk itself because I have other partitions on it that are running just fine. Makes me think that this is just yet another one of those several bugs in 10.04. 
I think im just going to revert back to 9.10 for a little while since that one was working perfectly for me and wait till all the issues in 10.04 get rectified.

### Post by badboy_2083 on 2010-05-29
I just got the same error again. It seems to happen right after a flash crash for me or maybe that is just coincidence.
